name: Bulbasaur
text:
[Pokemon Crystal] Bulbasaur (#001) — Grass/Poison Pokemon
Base Stats: HP 45 / Atk 49 / Def 49 / SpA 65 / SpD 65 / Spe 45 (BST 318)

Bulbasaur is a Grass/Poison-type Pokemon. Catch rate: 45. Growth rate: Medium Slow. Gender: 87.5% male. Egg groups: Monster, Grass. Hatch cycles: 20. No wild held items.

Evolution: Bulbasaur evolves into Ivysaur at level 16, then Venusaur at level 32.

As Grass/Poison, it is weak to Fire, Ice, Flying, and Psychic (2x each). It resists Water, Electric, Grass, and Fighting (0.5x each).

name: Bulbasaur
text:
[Pokemon Crystal] Bulbasaur — Level-Up Moves

Lv1 Tackle, Lv4 Growl, Lv7 Leech Seed, Lv10 Vine Whip, Lv15 PoisonPowder, Lv15 Sleep Powder, Lv20 Razor Leaf, Lv25 Sweet Scent, Lv32 Growth, Lv39 Synthesis, Lv46 SolarBeam.

Bulbasaur learns 11 moves by level-up. It gains STAB Grass moves at Lv10 (Vine Whip) and Lv20 (Razor Leaf). Its strongest level-up move is SolarBeam at Lv46.

name: Bulbasaur
text:
[Pokemon Crystal] Bulbasaur — TM/HM Compatibility

TMs: Headbutt, Curse, Toxic, Hidden Power, Sunny Day, Sweet Scent, Snore, Protect, Giga Drain, Endure, Frustration, SolarBeam, Return, Mud-Slap, Double Team, Swagger, Sleep Talk, Defense Curl, Rest, Attract, Fury Cutter.
HMs: Cut, Flash.

Notable: Giga Drain (STAB, reliable Grass damage), SolarBeam (powerful with Sunny Day), Toxic (synergy with Leech Seed stalling).

name: Bulbasaur
text:
[Pokemon Crystal] Bulbasaur — Egg Moves

Light Screen, Skull Bash, Safeguard, Razor Wind, Petal Dance.

Petal Dance is a strong STAB option (power 70, 2-3 turns, causes confusion after). Light Screen and Safeguard provide team support.

name: Bulbasaur
text:
[Pokemon Crystal] Evolution Chain: Bulbasaur → Ivysaur → Venusaur

Bulbasaur evolves into Ivysaur at level 16.
Ivysaur evolves into Venusaur at level 32.

This is a standard 3-stage level-based evolution line. All three share the Grass/Poison typing. BST progression: 318 → 405 → 525.

name: Cyndaquil
text:
[Pokemon Crystal] Cyndaquil (#155) — Fire Pokemon
Base Stats: HP 39 / Atk 52 / Def 43 / SpA 60 / SpD 50 / Spe 65 (BST 309)

Cyndaquil is one of the three Johto starter Pokemon. It is a pure Fire-type with good Speed and Special Attack. Catch rate: 45. Growth rate: Medium Slow. Gender: 87.5% male. Egg group: Field. Hatch cycles: 20.

Evolution: Cyndaquil → Quilava (Lv14) → Typhlosion (Lv36).

As a Fire type, it is weak to Water, Ground, and Rock. It resists Fire, Grass, Ice, Bug, and Steel.

name: Totodile
text:
[Pokemon Crystal] Totodile (#158) — Water Pokemon
Base Stats: HP 50 / Atk 65 / Def 64 / SpA 44 / SpD 48 / Spe 43 (BST 314)

Totodile is one of the three Johto starter Pokemon. Pure Water-type with high Attack for a starter. Catch rate: 45. Growth rate: Medium Slow. Gender: 87.5% male. Egg groups: Monster, Water 1. Hatch cycles: 20.

Evolution: Totodile → Croconaw (Lv18) → Feraligatr (Lv30).

As Water type, it is weak to Electric and Grass. It resists Fire, Water, Ice, and Steel.

name: Pikachu
text:
[Pokemon Crystal] Pikachu (#025) — Electric Pokemon
Base Stats: HP 35 / Atk 55 / Def 30 / SpA 50 / SpD 40 / Spe 90 (BST 300)

Pikachu is an Electric-type Pokemon. Catch rate: 190. Growth rate: Medium Fast. Gender: 50% male. Egg groups: Field, Fairy. Hatch cycles: 10.

Evolution: Pichu → Pikachu (happiness) → Raichu (Thunder Stone).

Special: Light Ball doubles Pikachu's Special Attack when held. Very fast (90 Spe) but fragile. Weak to Ground only. Immune to nothing. Resists Electric, Flying, and Steel.

name: Tyranitar
text:
[Pokemon Crystal] Tyranitar (#248) — Rock/Dark Pokemon
Base Stats: HP 100 / Atk 134 / Def 110 / SpA 95 / SpD 100 / Spe 61 (BST 600)

Tyranitar is the pseudo-legendary of Gen 2 with a massive 600 BST. Rock/Dark typing gives it unique offensive coverage. Catch rate: 45. Growth rate: Slow. Gender: 50% male. Egg group: Monster. Hatch cycles: 40.

Evolution: Larvitar (Lv30) → Pupitar (Lv55) → Tyranitar. Very late evolution.

Weaknesses: Fighting (4x!), Water, Grass, Ground, Bug, Steel (all 2x). Resists: Normal, Flying, Poison, Ghost, Fire, Dark. Immune to Psychic.

name: Dragonite
text:
[Pokemon Crystal] Dragonite (#149) — Dragon/Flying Pokemon
Base Stats: HP 91 / Atk 134 / Def 95 / SpA 100 / SpD 100 / Spe 80 (BST 600)

Dragonite is the Gen 1 pseudo-legendary with 600 BST. Dragon/Flying typing. Catch rate: 45. Growth rate: Slow. Gender: 50% male. Egg groups: Water 1, Dragon. Hatch cycles: 40.

Evolution: Dratini (Lv30) → Dragonair (Lv55) → Dragonite.

Used by Champion Lance (3 Dragonites). Weaknesses: Ice (4x!), Dragon, Rock (2x). Resists: Fire, Water, Grass, Fighting, Ground (immune), Bug.

name: Miltank
text:
[Pokemon Crystal] Miltank (#241) — Normal Pokemon
Base Stats: HP 95 / Atk 80 / Def 105 / SpA 40 / SpD 70 / Spe 100 (BST 490)

Miltank is a pure Normal-type known for Whitney's infamous gym battle. 100% female. Excellent physical bulk (105 Def) and Speed (100). Catch rate: 45. Growth rate: Slow. Egg group: Field. Hatch cycles: 20.

Weaknesses: Fighting (2x only). Immune to Ghost. No resistances.

Notable for Rollout + Milk Drink + Attract combination used by Gym Leader Whitney.

name: Lugia
text:
[Pokemon Crystal] Lugia (#249) — Psychic/Flying Legendary Pokemon
Base Stats: HP 106 / Atk 90 / Def 130 / SpA 90 / SpD 154 / Spe 110 (BST 680)

Lugia is a Legendary Pokemon found in the Whirl Islands. Requires Silver Wing. Psychic/Flying typing with extraordinary Special Defense (154). Catch rate: 3 (extremely hard to catch). Growth rate: Slow. Genderless. Cannot breed.

Weaknesses: Electric, Ice, Rock, Ghost, Dark (2x). Resists: Grass, Fighting, Psychic. Immune to Ground.

name: Thunderbolt
text:
[Pokemon Crystal] Move: Thunderbolt — Electric / Special / Power 95 / Acc 100% / PP 15

Thunderbolt is a strong, reliable Electric-type special attack. 10% chance to paralyze the target. Available as a Game Corner prize move tutor in Goldenrod City (Crystal only) and not a TM.

In Gen 2, all Electric moves are Special. Thunderbolt is the premier Electric attack — more accurate than Thunder (70%) with only slightly less power (95 vs 120).

name: Earthquake
text:
[Pokemon Crystal] Move: Earthquake — Ground / Physical / Power 100 / Acc 100% / PP 10

Earthquake is the premier Ground-type physical attack. No secondary effect. TM26, one of the best TMs in the game.

In Gen 2, Ground moves are Physical (use Attack stat). 100 power with 100% accuracy makes it one of the most reliable moves. Hits Pokemon using Dig for double damage. Cannot hit Flying-types or Pokemon using Fly.

name: Ice Beam
text:
[Pokemon Crystal] Move: Ice Beam — Ice / Special / Power 95 / Acc 100% / PP 10

Ice Beam is a powerful Ice-type special attack with 10% freeze chance. Available as a Game Corner prize move tutor in Goldenrod City (Crystal only).

In Gen 2, all Ice moves are Special. Excellent coverage move — super effective against Grass, Ground, Flying, and Dragon. The freeze chance, while low, can be game-changing since freeze has no natural thaw in Gen 2 (except being hit by a Fire move).

name: Rollout
text:
[Pokemon Crystal] Move: Rollout — Rock / Physical / Power 30 / Acc 90% / PP 20

Rollout attacks for 5 consecutive turns with doubling power each turn: 30 → 60 → 120 → 240 → 480. If Defense Curl was used beforehand, all Rollout damage is doubled (60 → 120 → 240 → 480 → 960). TM04.

The user cannot choose another move while Rollout is active. If it misses, the sequence resets. Infamous as Whitney's Miltank's signature strategy — Miltank uses Defense Curl then Rollout for devastating escalating damage.

name: Attract
text:
[Pokemon Crystal] Move: Attract — Normal / Status / Acc 100% / PP 15

Attract causes infatuation if the target is the opposite gender. An infatuated Pokemon has a 50% chance of being unable to attack each turn. Fails against same-gender or genderless Pokemon. TM45.

Key component of Whitney's Miltank strategy — since Miltank is always female, male Pokemon will be infatuated, combining with Rollout for a devastating combination.

name: Swords Dance
text:
[Pokemon Crystal] Move: Swords Dance — Normal / Status / Acc 100% / PP 30

Raises the user's Attack stat by 2 stages (doubles effective Attack). Not a TM in Gen 2. Learned by level-up or egg move only.

At +2 Attack, physical moves deal double damage. At +4 (two Swords Dances), they deal triple. Maximum is +6 (quadruple damage).

name: Toxic
text:
[Pokemon Crystal] Move: Toxic — Poison / Status / Acc 85% / PP 10

Badly poisons the target. Damage increases each turn: 1/16, 2/16, 3/16... of max HP. TM06 — one of the most widely distributed TMs, learned by almost every Pokemon.

Gen 2 bug: the badly poisoned counter resets when switching out, making Toxic less effective than intended. Poison-type Pokemon are immune. Steel-types are immune to Poison-type moves entirely.

name: Hidden Power
text:
[Pokemon Crystal] Move: Hidden Power — Normal (varies) / Special / Power varies / Acc 100% / PP 15

Hidden Power's type and power are determined by the Pokemon's DVs (IVs). Type can be any of the 17 types except Normal. Power ranges from 31 to 70. TM10.

In Gen 2, DVs range 0-15. The type is calculated from the low bits of Atk, Def, Spe, and Spc DVs. Power uses a different formula from all DVs. This makes Hidden Power type unpredictable but provides crucial coverage options.

name: Fly
text:
[Pokemon Crystal] Move: Fly — Flying / Physical / Power 70 / Acc 95% / PP 15

Two-turn move: user flies up on turn 1 (semi-invulnerable), attacks on turn 2. HM02. Also used as field move to fly to visited cities.

While airborne, most moves miss. However, Thunder and Twister can hit during Fly. Earthquake does NOT hit flying Pokemon (Ground immunity applies). Gust and Whirlwind also hit and deal double damage to airborne targets.

name: Leftovers
text:
[Pokemon Crystal] Item: Leftovers — Held Item

Restores 1/16 of the holder's max HP at the end of each turn. One of the best held items in the game for any Pokemon that expects to stay in for multiple turns.

Found on wild Snorlax (5% chance). The passive healing stacks with other recovery and can make bulky Pokemon very difficult to take down.

name: Quick Claw
text:
[Pokemon Crystal] Item: Quick Claw — Held Item

Gives the holder a 60% chance to move first in its priority bracket, regardless of Speed. Does not override move priority (e.g., Quick Attack still goes before a Quick Claw-boosted normal move).

Useful on slow, powerful Pokemon like Snorlax or Machamp to occasionally bypass their Speed disadvantage.

name: Focus Band
item_effect: 30% chance to survive fatal hit with 1 HP
text:
[Pokemon Crystal] Item: Focus Band — Held Item

When the holder would faint from damage, there is a ~11.7% chance (30/256) to survive with 1 HP instead. Not consumed on use — can activate multiple times.

Unreliable but can clutch games. Works on any damaging hit including multi-hit moves (checked per hit).

name: King's Rock
item_effect: Adds 30% flinch chance to damaging moves; trade evolution item
text:
[Pokemon Crystal] Item: King's Rock — Held Item

Adds a 30% flinch chance to damaging moves that don't already have a flinch effect. Also a trade evolution item: Poliwhirl holding King's Rock evolves into Politoed, Slowpoke holding it evolves into Slowking.

Flinch only works if the holder moves first. Stacks conceptually with multi-hit moves for more flinch chances.

name: Master Ball
text:
[Pokemon Crystal] Item: Master Ball — Poke Ball

Catches any wild Pokemon without fail. Only one is given in the game — received from Professor Elm after earning all 8 Johto badges.

Best saved for roaming legendary beasts (Raikou, Entei, Suicune) since they flee immediately, or for Lugia/Ho-Oh which have catch rate 3.

name: Charcoal
text:
[Pokemon Crystal] Item: Charcoal — Held Item

Boosts the power of the holder's Fire-type moves by 10%. Given by the Charcoal Maker in Azalea Town.

Type-boosting items provide a 1.1x multiplier to damage. Stacks multiplicatively with STAB (1.5x) for a total of 1.65x on same-type Fire moves.

name: Falkner
text:
[Pokemon Crystal] Trainer Battle: Gym Leader Falkner at Violet City
Team: Pidgey Lv7, Pidgeotto Lv9

Falkner is the first Gym Leader, specializing in Flying-types. Awards the Zephyr Badge (boosts Attack, enables Flash outside battle) and TM31 Mud-Slap.

Team:
- Lv7 Pidgey: Tackle, Mud-Slap
- Lv9 Pidgeotto: Tackle, Mud-Slap, Gust

Strategy: Rock-type Pokemon (Geodude from Dark Cave) wall his entire team. Electric types from Route 32 also work. His team is weak and Mud-Slap (lowers accuracy) is the main annoyance.

name: Whitney
text:
[Pokemon Crystal] Trainer Battle: Gym Leader Whitney at Goldenrod City
Team: Clefairy Lv18, Miltank Lv20

Whitney is the third Gym Leader and one of the most notoriously difficult early-game bosses. Awards the Plain Badge (boosts Speed, enables Strength) and TM45 Attract.

Team:
- Lv18 Clefairy: DoubleSlap, Mimic, Encore, Metronome
- Lv20 Miltank: Rollout, Attract, Stomp, Milk Drink

Miltank is the real threat. Rollout escalates in power each turn (30→60→120→240→480). Attract causes infatuation on male Pokemon (50% unable to attack). Milk Drink fully heals. Stomp has 30% flinch. Fighting-types like Machop (trade in Goldenrod Dept Store) or female Pokemon (immune to Attract) are key counters.

name: Morty
text:
[Pokemon Crystal] Trainer Battle: Gym Leader Morty at Ecruteak City
Team: Gastly Lv21, Haunter Lv21, Gengar Lv25, Haunter Lv23

Morty is the fourth Gym Leader, specializing in Ghost-types. Awards the Fog Badge (enables Surf) and TM30 Shadow Ball.

Team:
- Lv21 Gastly: Lick, Spite, Mean Look, Curse
- Lv21 Haunter: Hypnosis, Mimic, Curse, Night Shade
- Lv25 Gengar: Hypnosis, Shadow Ball, Mean Look, Dream Eater
- Lv23 Haunter: Spite, Mean Look, Mimic, Night Shade

Gengar is the ace — Hypnosis + Dream Eater combo is dangerous. Mean Look prevents switching. All are Ghost/Poison, so Normal and Fighting moves are immune. Use Dark-type moves (Bite, Faint Attack) or Psychic. A Pokemon with Insomnia or high Special Defense helps.

name: Clair
text:
[Pokemon Crystal] Trainer Battle: Gym Leader Clair at Blackthorn City
Team: Dragonair Lv37, Dragonair Lv37, Dragonair Lv37, Kingdra Lv40

Clair is the eighth and final Johto Gym Leader. Awards the Rising Badge (all Pokemon obey, enables Waterfall). Does NOT give badge immediately — requires Dragon's Den test first.

Team:
- Lv37 Dragonair: Thunder Wave, Surf, Slam, DragonBreath
- Lv37 Dragonair: Thunder Wave, Thunderbolt, Slam, DragonBreath
- Lv37 Dragonair: Thunder Wave, Ice Beam, Slam, DragonBreath
- Lv40 Kingdra: SmokeScreen, Surf, Hyper Beam, DragonBreath

Kingdra (Water/Dragon) has only one weakness: Dragon. Each Dragonair has different coverage. Ice-type moves work on Dragonair but not Kingdra. Bring diverse coverage and Dragon-type attacks if available.

name: Lance
text:
[Pokemon Crystal] Trainer Battle: Champion Lance at Indigo Plateau
Team: Gyarados Lv44, Dragonite Lv47, Dragonite Lv47, Charizard Lv46, Aerodactyl Lv46, Dragonite Lv50

Lance is the Pokemon League Champion. His team is Dragon-themed but includes Gyarados, Charizard, and Aerodactyl for coverage.

- Lv44 Gyarados: Surf, Rain Dance, Hyper Beam, DragonBreath
- Lv47 Dragonite: Thunder Wave, Twister, Thunder, Hyper Beam
- Lv47 Dragonite: Thunder Wave, Blizzard, Twister, Hyper Beam
- Lv46 Charizard: Flamethrower, Wing Attack, Slash, Hyper Beam
- Lv46 Aerodactyl: Wing Attack, AncientPower, Rock Slide, Hyper Beam
- Lv50 Dragonite: Fire Blast, Safeguard, Outrage, Hyper Beam

Ice-type moves are crucial — 4x effective against all 3 Dragonites. Electric handles Gyarados. Rock moves hit most of his team.

name: Will
text:
[Pokemon Crystal] Trainer Battle: Elite Four Will at Indigo Plateau
Team: Xatu Lv40, Jynx Lv41, Exeggutor Lv41, Slowbro Lv41, Xatu Lv42

Will is the first Elite Four member, specializing in Psychic-types.

- Lv40 Xatu: Quick Attack, Future Sight, Confuse Ray, Psychic
- Lv41 Jynx: DoubleSlap, Lovely Kiss, Ice Punch, Psychic
- Lv41 Exeggutor: Reflect, Leech Seed, Egg Bomb, Psychic
- Lv41 Slowbro: Curse, Amnesia, Body Slam, Psychic
- Lv42 Xatu: Quick Attack, Future Sight, Confuse Ray, Psychic

Dark-types wall his whole team (immune to Psychic). Bug and Ghost moves are also super effective. Watch out for Jynx's Lovely Kiss (sleep).

name: Rival (Azalea Town)
text:
[Pokemon Crystal] Trainer Battle: Rival at Azalea Town
Team varies by player's starter choice. Rival always has the starter with type advantage.

If player chose Chikorita: Lv12 Gastly, Lv14 Zubat, Lv16 Quilava
If player chose Cyndaquil: Lv12 Gastly, Lv14 Zubat, Lv16 Croconaw
If player chose Totodile: Lv12 Gastly, Lv14 Zubat, Lv16 Bayleef

Second rival battle. All three variants include Gastly and Zubat as common members. The rival's starter will be the type advantage over the player's choice.

name: Route 29
text:
[Pokemon Crystal] Wild Encounters: Route 29 — Grass

Morning: Pidgey (Lv2-3), Sentret (Lv2-3)
Day: Pidgey (Lv2-3), Sentret (Lv2-3)
Night: Hoothoot (Lv2-3), Rattata (Lv2-3)

Route 29 connects New Bark Town (east) to Cherrygrove City (west). It is the first route in the game with no trainers. Morning and day encounters are Pidgey/Sentret, while night switches to Hoothoot/Rattata. All Pokemon are levels 2-3.

name: Route 32
text:
[Pokemon Crystal] Wild Encounters: Route 32 — Grass

Morning: Bellsprout (Lv4-6), Ekans (Lv4-6), Mareep (Lv6), Hoppip (Lv5-7)
Day: Bellsprout (Lv4-6), Ekans (Lv4-6), Mareep (Lv6), Hoppip (Lv5-7)
Night: Bellsprout (Lv4-6), Wooper (Lv4), Gastly (Lv6), Zubat (Lv4)

Route 32 connects Violet City (north) to Union Cave (south). Long route with several trainers. Mareep (Electric-type) is only available morning/day and is very useful for upcoming gym battles.

name: Dark Cave (Violet City entrance)
text:
[Pokemon Crystal] Wild Encounters: Dark Cave (Violet City side) — Cave

All times: Geodude (Lv2-4), Zubat (Lv2-4)

Dark Cave is accessible early from Route 31 near Violet City. Geodude is a key early catch — Rock/Ground typing walls Falkner's Flying team completely. Requires Flash (HM05) to see, but encounters work without it.

name: Mt. Silver
text:
[Pokemon Crystal] Wild Encounters: Mt. Silver — Cave/Exterior

Mt. Silver is the final dungeon, accessible only after getting all 16 badges. Wild Pokemon include Larvitar (Lv20, rare), Golduck (Lv20-25), Misdreavus (Lv20, night), Ursaring (Lv30-40), Donphan (Lv30-40), and Sneasel (Lv30).

Larvitar is found only here — the base form of pseudo-legendary Tyranitar. Trainer Red awaits at the summit with a Lv81 Pikachu.

name: Fire
text:
[Pokemon Crystal] Type Matchup: Fire — Offensive Summary

Super effective (2x) against: Grass, Ice, Bug, Steel
Not very effective (0.5x) against: Fire, Water, Rock, Dragon
No effect (0x) against: none

Fire is a strong offensive type, hitting 4 types super effectively including the common Grass and Bug types, and the new Steel type. Its main drawback is being resisted by Water and Rock, both common defensive types. In Gen 2, all Fire moves are Special (use Special Attack stat).

name: Fire
text:
[Pokemon Crystal] Type Matchup: Fire — Defensive Summary

Weak to (2x from): Water, Ground, Rock
Resists (0.5x from): Fire, Grass, Ice, Bug, Steel
Immune to (0x from): none

Fire has 5 resistances but 3 common weaknesses (Water, Ground, Rock — all very common attacking types). Fire-types benefit greatly from Sunny Day (weakens Water moves, boosts own Fire moves).

name: Electric
text:
[Pokemon Crystal] Type Matchup: Electric — Offensive Summary

Super effective (2x) against: Water, Flying
Not very effective (0.5x) against: Electric, Grass, Dragon
No effect (0x) against: Ground

Electric hits Water and Flying super effectively — both very common types. Its main limitation is the total immunity from Ground-types. In Gen 2, all Electric moves are Special. Electric-type coverage is almost mandatory for any team due to the prevalence of Water-types.

name: Dragon
text:
[Pokemon Crystal] Type Matchup: Dragon — Offensive Summary

Super effective (2x) against: Dragon
Not very effective (0.5x) against: Steel
No effect (0x) against: none

Dragon is one of the worst offensive types in Gen 2 — it only hits Dragon super effectively and is resisted by Steel. The only Dragon-type damaging moves in Gen 2 are DragonBreath (60 power, 30% paralyze) and Outrage (120 power, 2-3 turns, confusion after). Dragon-types are better off using Ice Beam or other coverage.

name: Fighting
text:
[Pokemon Crystal] Type Matchup: Fighting — Offensive Summary

Super effective (2x) against: Normal, Ice, Rock, Dark, Steel
Not very effective (0.5x) against: Poison, Flying, Psychic, Bug
No effect (0x) against: Ghost

Fighting is the best offensive type in Gen 2, hitting 5 types super effectively. It is the primary way to deal with Normal, Rock, Dark, and Steel types. In Gen 2, all Fighting moves are Physical. Key Fighting moves: DynamicPunch (100 power, 50% acc, confuses), Cross Chop (100 power, high crit).

name: Ice
text:
[Pokemon Crystal] Type Matchup: Ice — Offensive Summary

Super effective (2x) against: Grass, Ground, Flying, Dragon
Not very effective (0.5x) against: Fire, Water, Ice, Steel
No effect (0x) against: none

Ice is an excellent offensive type, hitting 4 types super effectively including the important Dragon and Flying types. In Gen 2, Ice moves are Special. Ice Beam (95 power, 10% freeze) is one of the most important coverage moves in the game. Ice's defensive typing is terrible (only resists itself), so Ice is best used as coverage on non-Ice-types.

name: Violet City
text:
[Pokemon Crystal] Location: Violet City — Johto City
Connections: Route 31 (west), Route 32 (south), Route 36 (west)

Violet City is the first major city with a gym. Features:
- Violet City Gym (Falkner, Flying-type, Zephyr Badge)
- Sprout Tower (training dungeon, receive HM05 Flash from Elder)
- Pokemon Center, Poke Mart
- Earl's Pokemon Academy
- Trade NPC: Bellsprout for Onix (Rocky)

The player must defeat Falkner to progress south to Route 32 and Azalea Town. Sprout Tower is optional but recommended for experience and Flash HM.

name: Goldenrod City
text:
[Pokemon Crystal] Location: Goldenrod City — Johto City
Connections: Route 34 (south), Route 35 (north)

Goldenrod City is the largest city in Johto and the game's central hub. Features:
- Goldenrod Gym (Whitney, Normal-type, Plain Badge)
- Goldenrod Dept Store (6 floors, largest shop)
- Radio Tower (Team Rocket takeover later)
- Game Corner (slot machines, prize Pokemon/TMs; Crystal: move tutors for Thunderbolt/Ice Beam/Flamethrower)
- Name Rater, Bike Shop (free Bicycle)
- Underground Warehouse (Haircut Brothers, shop)
- Global Terminal (trade/battle)
- Bill's house (PC Box upgrade)

Major story location — Radio Tower takeover occurs after Mahogany Town events.

name: Route 34
text:
[Pokemon Crystal] Location: Route 34 — Johto Route
Connections: Goldenrod City (north), Ilex Forest (south)

Route 34 connects Ilex Forest to Goldenrod City. Features:
- Day Care Center (breed Pokemon, egg hatching)
- Several trainers (Youngsters, Lasses, Cooltrainers)
- Surfing area with wild Tentacool/Tentacruel

The Day Care is one of the most important facilities in the game for breeding Pokemon and obtaining egg moves.

name: Receive Starter Pokemon
text:
[Pokemon Crystal] Story: Receive Starter Pokemon
Location: New Bark Town — Elm's Lab | Prerequisites: none

The game begins in New Bark Town. The player visits Professor Elm's lab and chooses one of three starter Pokemon:
- Chikorita (Grass) — defensive, harder early game (Falkner and Bugsy resist Grass)
- Cyndaquil (Fire) — offensive Special attacker, easiest early game
- Totodile (Water) — physical attacker, balanced difficulty

After choosing, Elm asks the player to visit Mr. Pokemon on Route 30 to pick up a Mystery Egg.

name: First Rival Battle
text:
[Pokemon Crystal] Story: First Rival Battle
Location: Cherrygrove City (outside) | Prerequisites: Visit Mr. Pokemon

After receiving the Mystery Egg from Mr. Pokemon on Route 30, Professor Elm calls about a break-in. Returning to New Bark Town, the player encounters the Rival outside Cherrygrove City.

The Rival has stolen the starter Pokemon with type advantage over the player's choice. This is a 1v1 battle against a Lv5 Pokemon. After winning, the player returns to Elm's Lab, names the Rival, and receives Poke Balls.

name: Team Rocket Radio Tower Takeover
text:
[Pokemon Crystal] Story: Team Rocket Radio Tower Takeover
Location: Goldenrod City Radio Tower | Prerequisites: Clear Mahogany Rocket Hideout

After defeating Team Rocket at their hideout under the Mahogany Town shop and clearing the Lake of Rage Red Gyarados event, Team Rocket takes over the Goldenrod Radio Tower.

The player must infiltrate the Radio Tower (5 floors of Rocket Grunts and Executives), obtain the Basement Key, clear the Underground Warehouse, rescue the Director, and defeat the final Rocket Executive. This permanently disbands Team Rocket. Rewards: Clear Bell (needed for Tin Tower/Ho-Oh encounter).

name: Damage Formula
formula_text: Damage = (((2 * Level / 5 + 2) * Power * A / D / 50) + 2) * Modifiers
text:
[Pokemon Crystal] Battle Mechanic: Damage Formula

Base damage = (((2 * Level / 5 + 2) * Power * A / D / 50) + 2)

Variables:
- Level = attacker's level
- Power = move's base power
- A = attacker's effective Attack (physical) or Special Attack (special)
- D = defender's effective Defense (physical) or Special Defense (special)

Physical vs Special is determined by move TYPE in Gen 2:
- Physical: Normal, Fighting, Flying, Poison, Ground, Rock, Bug, Ghost, Steel
- Special: Fire, Water, Electric, Grass, Ice, Psychic, Dragon, Dark

The base damage is then multiplied by modifiers in order:
1. Item boost (1.1x if holding type-boosting item)
2. Critical hit (2x)
3. TK (Triple Kick only: 1x, 2x, 3x)
4. Weather (1.5x boost or 0.5x reduction)
5. Badge boost (1.125x if player has relevant badge)
6. STAB (1.5x if move type matches user's type)
7. Type effectiveness (0x, 0.25x, 0.5x, 1x, 2x, 4x)
8. Move-specific modifiers (Rollout escalation, etc.)
9. Random (217-255 / 255, approximately 85-100%)
10. Pursuit double damage (2x if target is switching)

name: Damage Modifiers Detail
text:
[Pokemon Crystal] Battle Mechanic: Damage Modifiers — Detail & Edge Cases

Critical Hit & Stat Stages:
When a critical hit lands, if the defender's Def/SpDef stage >= attacker's Atk/SpAtk stage, ALL stat stage modifiers are ignored, burn's attack halving is ignored, and Reflect/Light Screen are ignored. Otherwise, stat stages apply normally. This means crits don't always ignore stat changes — only when they would be unfavorable to the attacker.

Weather:
- Rain: Water moves 1.5x, Fire moves 0.5x. Thunder accuracy becomes 100%.
- Sun: Fire moves 1.5x, Water moves 0.5x. SolarBeam fires instantly.
- Sandstorm: 1/8 max HP damage per turn to all non-Rock/Ground/Steel types.

Badge boosts:
- Zephyr Badge: 1.125x Attack
- Other badges boost other stats similarly
- Only applies to player-owned Pokemon, never opponent's

Random factor: Integer from 217-255, divided by 255. Minimum damage roll is ~85% of max.

name: Critical Hit System
text:
[Pokemon Crystal] Battle Mechanic: Critical Hit System

Critical hit rate in Gen 2 is based on the user's base Speed stat:

Stage 0 (normal): BaseSpeed / 512
Stage 1 (high-crit move like Slash): BaseSpeed / 256
Stage 2 (Focus Energy or Scope Lens): BaseSpeed / 128
Stage 3 (high-crit + Focus Energy): BaseSpeed / 64

Critical hits deal 2x damage. Maximum possible crit rate is ~25% at stage 3 with high base Speed.

High-crit moves: Slash, Karate Chop, Razor Leaf, Crabhammer, Cross Chop, Aeroblast.

Items: Scope Lens (+1 crit stage), Lucky Punch (Chansey only, +2), Stick (Farfetch'd only, +2).

Focus Energy raises crit stage by 1 (it was bugged in Gen 1 but fixed in Gen 2).

name: STAB (Same-Type Attack Bonus)
text:
[Pokemon Crystal] Battle Mechanic: STAB (Same-Type Attack Bonus)

If a Pokemon uses a move that matches one of its types, the move's damage is multiplied by 1.5x. This is called STAB.

Example: Typhlosion (Fire-type) using Flamethrower (Fire move) gets 1.5x damage. Typhlosion using Earthquake (Ground move) does not.

Dual-typed Pokemon get STAB on both types. STAB only applies to damaging moves (not status moves). STAB stacks with type-boosting items (1.5 * 1.1 = 1.65x total).

name: Burn Status
text:
[Pokemon Crystal] Battle Mechanic: Burn Status

Burn has two effects:
1. Deals 1/8 of the burned Pokemon's max HP as damage at the end of each turn
2. Halves the burned Pokemon's Attack stat (physical damage is halved)

Burn cannot affect Fire-type Pokemon. The Attack reduction makes burn devastating for physical attackers. Burn damage is fixed at 1/8 max HP regardless of the source.

Cured by: Burn Heal, Full Heal, Full Restore, Ice Berry (held, auto-cure), Heal Bell. Rest cures burn but puts the user to sleep.

name: Freeze Status
text:
[Pokemon Crystal] Battle Mechanic: Freeze Status

Freeze completely prevents the frozen Pokemon from acting. In Gen 2, there is NO random thaw chance per turn (unlike later gens). A frozen Pokemon stays frozen until:
1. Hit by a Fire-type move (thaws and takes damage)
2. Uses a thawing move: Flame Wheel or Sacred Fire (if the frozen Pokemon selected it before freezing, it will thaw and execute)
3. Cured by item: Ice Heal, Full Heal, Full Restore, Burnt Berry (held, auto-cure)
4. Rest cures freeze
5. Heal Bell from a teammate

Freeze is the most powerful status in Gen 2 because it has no natural recovery. Ice-type Pokemon cannot be frozen.

name: Rain Dance Weather
text:
[Pokemon Crystal] Battle Mechanic: Rain Dance Weather

Rain Dance sets rain for 5 turns. Effects:
- Water-type moves deal 1.5x damage
- Fire-type moves deal 0.5x damage
- Thunder accuracy becomes 100% (normally 70%)
- SolarBeam power is halved (60 instead of 120) and still requires charge turn
- Moonlight/Morning Sun restore 1/4 max HP instead of 1/2

Rain benefits Water-types enormously and cripples Fire-types. The Thunder accuracy boost makes Thunder + Rain Dance a powerful combo on any Pokemon that learns both.

name: Stat Stage System
text:
[Pokemon Crystal] Battle Mechanic: Stat Stage System

Each stat (Atk, Def, SpA, SpD, Spe) can be modified by stages from -6 to +6:

| Stage | Multiplier |
|-------|------------|
| -6    | 2/8 (0.25x) |
| -5    | 2/7 (0.29x) |
| -4    | 2/6 (0.33x) |
| -3    | 2/5 (0.40x) |
| -2    | 2/4 (0.50x) |
| -1    | 2/3 (0.67x) |
| 0     | 2/2 (1.00x) |
| +1    | 3/2 (1.50x) |
| +2    | 4/2 (2.00x) |
| +3    | 5/2 (2.50x) |
| +4    | 6/2 (3.00x) |
| +5    | 7/2 (3.50x) |
| +6    | 8/2 (4.00x) |

Accuracy and Evasion use separate stage tables. Stat stages reset when switching out. Baton Pass preserves stat stages when switching.

name: Move Priority System
text:
[Pokemon Crystal] Battle Mechanic: Move Priority System

Moves execute in priority order. Higher priority goes first. Within the same priority bracket, the faster Pokemon moves first.

Priority brackets in Gen 2:
+2: none in Gen 2
+1: Mach Punch, Quick Attack, ExtremeSpeed, Protect, Detect, Endure
 0: Most moves (default priority)
-1: Vital Throw
-3: Counter, Mirror Coat (go last in bracket, need to be hit first)
-5: Roar, Whirlwind (always go last)

Speed ties: broken randomly (50/50). Quick Claw: 60% chance to move first within the same priority bracket, overriding Speed. Switching always happens before any move.

name: Capture Formula
text:
[Pokemon Crystal] Battle Mechanic: Capture Formula

Capture rate calculation:
1. Modified catch rate = Species catch rate * Ball modifier * Status modifier
   - Ball modifiers: Poke Ball (1x), Great Ball (1.5x), Ultra Ball (2x), Master Ball (auto-catch)
   - Status: Sleep/Freeze (2x), Poison/Burn/Paralysis (1.5x)
2. Shake check: Random 0-255. If random < modified catch rate, one shake succeeds.
3. Four successful shakes = caught.

Apricorn Balls have special modifiers:
- Heavy Ball: +/- based on weight
- Level Ball: bonus if your level > wild level
- Lure Ball: 3x when fishing
- Moon Ball: intended for Moon Stone evolutions (bugged — checks for Burn Heal instead)
- Love Ball: 8x for same species opposite gender
- Fast Ball: intended for fleeing Pokemon (bugged — checks wrong byte)
- Friend Ball: sets caught Pokemon's happiness to 200

name: How to Beat Whitney
text:
[Pokemon Crystal] Strategy Guide: How to Beat Whitney's Miltank

Whitney's Miltank (Lv20) is one of the hardest early-game fights. Its strategy: Attract (infatuates male Pokemon) → Rollout (escalating damage) → Stomp (flinch) → Milk Drink (full heal).

Counter strategies:
1. USE A FEMALE POKEMON — Immune to Attract. Female Geodude from Dark Cave is ideal (Rock resists Rollout, Stomp).
2. TRADE FOR MACHOP — In Goldenrod Dept Store, trade Drowzee for Machop. Fighting is super effective vs Normal, and the trade boost gives extra EXP.
3. BREAK ROLLOUT — Any move that causes Miltank to flinch, sleep, or miss interrupts Rollout's escalation. Headbutt (30% flinch) or Sleep Powder/Hypnosis work.
4. GASTLY — Immune to Stomp and Rollout (Normal and Rock are both resisted/immune). Use Curse to whittle it down, or Hypnosis.
5. LEVEL UP — Grind to Lv22+ on Route 34/35. Raw stats can overpower Miltank.

Defeat Clefairy first (easy), then focus all resources on Miltank.

name: Johto Story Team Building
text:
[Pokemon Crystal] Strategy Guide: Johto Story Mode Team Building

Key type coverage to aim for: Water, Electric, Fire/Grass, Fighting, Ice, Flying/Ground.

Recommended early catches by gym progression:
- Geodude (Dark Cave, pre-Falkner): Rock/Ground, walls Falkner, good through mid-game
- Mareep (Route 32, morning/day): Electric, evolves Ampharos, one of the best Gen 2 Pokemon
- Heracross (Headbutt trees, Azalea): Bug/Fighting, incredibly strong, beats Whitney easily
- Gastly (Sprout Tower at night): Ghost/Poison, great for Morty and Whitney

If Cyndaquil starter: pick up a Water type (Wooper Route 32 night, or Poliwag Good Rod later)
If Totodile starter: Grass coverage via Oddish/Bellsprout Route 34-35, or just rely on Electric
If Chikorita starter: You need Fire coverage — Vulpix (Route 36) or Growlithe (Route 35-36)

Must-have TMs: Earthquake (Victory Road), Ice Beam (Game Corner), Thunderbolt (Game Corner)
